speaker: interviewer
text: Three. You said that you were reaching Uzbekistan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, by now we were reaching Uzbekistan and into Samarkand. And we came to Samarkand. And, again, we didn't have where to stay and what to eat. And we chose to go into a [? co-op, ?] an Uzbek co-op. Only Uzbeks were there-- Muslims, by the way. And-- and we stayed there. We were the only Polish family. No, there was another Polish family. Like, Jew-- two Jewish Polish families were there, without the men. Only the other lady had also two children. And we were two.

speaker: interviewee
text: So conditions were horrible-- no food, nothing. There was-- once a week we should-- we were supposed to get some supplies. And they were-- they were no supplies. I was always going with my mother and-- into that distribution place. And while my mother was walking around with that guy that was in charge of giving us the supplies, I took a private tour of the place. And I soon find out that there is some stuff that I can put in my pockets. So-- that was-- would help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the things was they-- there was a very hard cheese, that was like-- like a golf ball-- that hard. But when you soak it for a long time, you know, you had some nourishment of it. So I figured out that [? that's it. ?] So I used to go with a few-- with stuff that had a lot of pockets. And I used to fill up my pockets and put even in-- in my hat-- fill it up. My mother used to scream at me. Always she used to say, Moshe, if he catches you, you are dead. He'll kill you. And I won't be able to help you. But starvation is stronger than fear. And-- and I knew that is the only way that I can help somehow, you know, for ours-- for us, for my brother, and me. And so I kept on doing it, and quite a while, till one day he found-- he found me. He saw me. And he almost killed me. So after that, we couldn't stay anymore. Because it was-- we were starving there. There is another incident that I want to mention. Before we even reached Samarkand, on the way, we stopped in a place. And we were weeks, and weeks, and weeks on the-- on the way. It was no food and no supplies. And the place that we stopped-- they were a lot of refugees, a lot of people that are-- it was in Russia, on the way to-- to Uzbekistan. And we came to that place. And once a day, they had there-- they were handing out coupons for a meal, but only for a-- for a couple of-- for a short time during the day. And since they were thousands of people standing in the line, I used to go always with my mother. And we were standing in the line. And I said to myself, there is no way we'll ever get anything out of standing here in the line. So I said to my mother, I am going for a walk. You stay here. And I went for a walk. And I surveyed the-- this building. There was-- it was a small building. He was sitting in the-- in the window, at a small window. And he was sitting-- it probably wasn't too high. Because people could reach, and stand in, and you know. And he was giving out those coupons for a meal. I surveyed the building. And I saw, on the other side of the building, there was a window with a broken glass-- broken window. And I figured, I can climb this window.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I climbed in. And I came through that window, into that room that he was sitting. He was sitting all by himself. And I reached behind him. And I knocked on his back. And here were thousands of people-- thousands standing and waiting for that coupon to get. And he looked around. And he says, what are you doing here? Get out of it, or I call the police. I said, I don't care. You're can call whoever you want-- even Stalin himself. I am not leaving here till I get my coupons. I have a younger brother with me, and my mother, and we didn't see food for two weeks. So if I don't get your-- the coupons, I am not leaving. I guess he-- he liked my-- my courage. And he gave me those coupons for a meal. And he says, you know what? From now on, every day you come in the same way, and you'll get your coupon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for the time we were there, for-- I don't know how long it was-- I was getting this way, and getting the coupons. I got my coupons. I got to my-- I got to my mother. She was standing still in line. I said, come. I have the coupons. I showed her the coupons. We have a meal for today. And I told her the story. So that was one incident, how children stop to being children, but providers. And so we-- we were in that [NON-ENGLISH], and we decide-- outside Samarkand. And we decided that we were starving there. And we came back to Samarkand. We came back to Samarkand. And we were looking for a place to be. And to the-- at that point, we were sleeping on-- on the streets. And in one incident, we had a small duffel bag-- I guess it was-- with all our belongings there-- a little bit something to dress and one picture of my parents' wedding. That was the only picture that we had of our father. And during the night, even so, we were sleeping on it, and protecting it, and holding on to it. And one instant, we felt that it was pulled out. And it was gone. And that's it. Because there were thousands of people sleeping around us. So that was the end of that picture, which we were sorry about, more than anything else. Because no money, and no-- and not much other stuff in it, anyways too. OK, then we hear that-- so my mother figured, OK, there are some orphanages. And I can't-- she doesn't have what to feed us. And if she won't feed us, we will probably starve in her hands. So she'll give us up in an orphanage. And at least we'll have where to sleep and what to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was '42. So I was nine years old. And my brother was seven. So we came-- so we went to a Russian orphanage, you know. So we came to a Russian orphanage And they said that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mother stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, meantime we all stayed on the-- on the street. We-- we-- we slept on the street. We lived on the street, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to the orphanage, where did your mother go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't get yet to the orphanage. We were just trying to get into it. But once-- so we came to that Russian orphanage. And they said they have only room for one child, not for two. So, OK. So my mother said, OK. So she put my brother in that orphanage. And we were going to look for another orphanage to put me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way we left my brother, we met some people, you know. You go. You meet all your people around. And you start to talk, and to ask. And my mother said, I just left one of my boys into that Russian orphanage. She says-- the people say, what did you do? You-- you lost your son. It's finished. You will never get him back. And then they told us all kind of stories. So we run back to that orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, he was gone. We couldn't-- we couldn't find him anymore. It took us hours, and hours, and hours. He was sent already from one place to another, and then to-- but finally, we didn't give up. We were running from one place to the other, till we-- till we found him, in one orphanage. But by the time you are there, inside, you are their property. You're-- it's-- it's not your son, or whatever. You are their property. We couldn't get him even out. After a lot of arguments and that, we got him out in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we hear that there is an orphanage from the Polish government in exile, in [INAUDIBLE], the day-- but the problem there was that they didn't want to accept Jewish children. They mainly accepted Polish children. But since we didn't have what to lose, we had to go through an interview, and a doctor, and that-- so my mother said, OK. We'll go and see. So we went there. And he checked us out. And we were in very poor condition. And he agreed to take us back.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we were in such poor condition-- mainly me-- that when I reached that Polish orphanage-- I was, as I said, nine years old-- I couldn't walk anymore. They had to carry me. And I was-- my legs and hands were so thin that they gave me a nickname, which stayed to me all during the years till I reached Israel-- Palestine. And the nickname was [POLISH], which means a match, in Polish. So that-- that is how-- how skinny I was. OK, so we reached that-- we were accepted to that orphanage. At least we had a place where to sleep and to eat. And we made a point-- I told my brother, OK, each one got a-- a little piece-- in the morning, a little piece of bread. So I said-- and something to drink-- so I said to my brother, OK, we'll-- we'll eat both together one piece. We'll share one piece. And one piece we'll-- we'll-- we'll bring out-- we'll give our mother. Because she used to come every day, in the morning, to visit us at the gate. So one piece of bread we used to give her. Then she disappeared for a while. And we didn't know what happened. Turned out that she went to another place where to stay, because she didn't have where to stay. And she didn't have what to eat. And she went to a kind of another [NON-ENGLISH]. It so happened that in that other [NON-ENGLISH], a dog bit her very-- very badly. And they had to bring her back to Samarkand. And she was put in a hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- also, there was a Polish hospital, already from the Polish government, that she opened in Samarkand. So she was put there, in that hospital. And after she recovered, she came to visit us. And this how we found out where she disappeared, how she disappeared. And in that hospital, she stayed, just-- she worked there, just to have a meal a day and somewhere to put her head down, you know? What was it like for you at the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was some antisemitism that we-- that we countered there. We had to be with every day in the-- every day in the morning, we had to stand together, with all Polish children. They used to do their prayers. And we had to be there. They didn't force us to pray. But we had to stay with-- there, in the same room, and [? yeah. ?] And this is how I learned all the-- all the prayer, all the Polish prayer, all the Catholic prayers. And we stayed for a while in that orphanage. I guess it wasn't more than some months, till they told us that we are leaving Russia for Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like in the orphanage? Sleeping conditions, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was one huge hole-- huge hole-- where-- and I don't know if they were beds. I-- I think maybe they were beds, one beside the other-- one huge hole, where all the children were sleeping, one-- one beside the other. I think they were over-- I think 150 children in that orphanage. We were fed-- I mean, not overfed, but you know. Under those conditions, it was like heaven, you know. And-- and the teachers were very nice-- very nice. I remember I loved our taker-- caretakers. They liked me. And-- and we were generally happy there, you know. After a-- after some months, we-- we hear-- they told us that we are leaving Russia for Palestine. We were so excited. This was-- we were so happy that it was unbelievable. So we-- we started our journey to-- to-- to Palestine. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother came with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother didn't. She wanted. She wanted-- she couldn't come with us. Because they took only children. But she wanted to get herself into that plane, and to that train, and you know-- and hide herself, and travel with us, and that-- but I was very afraid. I said, no. I don't want you. If you-- if you do it, and they-- and they find, they-- they-- they can kill you, send you to jail. And who knows what will happen? I-- I-- I am very afraid-- I don't want you. I don't want you. I am scared. I don't want you. So she didn't come. She stayed in-- in Samarkand. But she stayed in that hospital. And that's how we-- for-- for those years, we managed to-- to be in contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we left in-- we left '42. We left Russia to Iran. We-- our first stop in Iran was a place-- when we got off the boat-- was a place that was called-- called Pahlavi. It was-- I think it was-- the shah's name was [? Lavi. ?] So that was after him named, the shah's family. So-- and from there, we were taken by trucks to Tehran. And there, we had an-- a camp. They-- they built a camp. They made a camp, a tent-- from tent, in Tehran.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed. And we were hoping, originally, to get through Baghdad to Jordan, and to Palestine. But for political reasons, it didn't work out. And so we waited and waited. And since we were the first group that arrived in Tehran, and we-- so we-- and they were waiting, waiting-- by the time we left Tehran, we were about 800 children or-- or more. And we left Tehran. And we left to India. We were in-- we came to India, to Karachi. And we stayed there some-- some weeks in Karachi. And we-- by boat we came. We reached in India by boat to Karachi. And then we came through the Swiss canal, from Karachi. We came to Ismailia, to-- to Suez Canal. And with the train, we came through the Sinai, to Palestine. And we reached Palestine. It was 19 of February, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the reception was unbelievable. Because all over the-- the route, from-- from the Sinai-- I mean, once we reached into Palestine, from Gaza and farther on, we went all the way to-- through Haifa to, [? Aklit. ?] There was a-- a camp, [? Aklit ?] called. And all through the way, the people were lining up, and cheering, and bringing us oranges, which was something that we never saw before, and-- and fruits, and food, and everything. And-- and we reached-- we reached [? Aklit. ?] In [? Aklit, ?] since we were so many children, and with us they came also some families, too. So there were well over 1,000 people. And facilities-- at that time, Palestine was a small Jewish community. It was just about 600,000 Jews or less. So they had to split so many children, all over the country, to divide into all different facilities. So they split us all over-- all kind of facilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet up with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother, I met up in 19-- by the end of 1947. She left Russia in '45, after the war. And then-- and the Russian-- the Russian government allowed the Polish people to leave-- the Polish citizens to leave Russia for Poland. So they all left. And they came to Poland. And from Poland, they continued to Germany. And from Germany, she came to Palestine. It was still-- she still reached, by the end of 1947, before Israel got independence. But it was already after the declaration in the United Nations, in Lake Success, that they-- that they declared of a partition of Palestine for two states-- a Jewish state and a Arab state.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war ended, was it something that you talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What exactly do you mean? What--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss your experiences after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. You know, I-- I grew up, like as I mentioned, from '42 till the end of '48-- from beginning of '42 to the end of '48. I was always-- I grew up in orphanages, or [NON-ENGLISH], or with children. And we-- here and there, one would tell a story there. But we didn't really mention it much. You know, we hear the little story there, a little story here. The-- but we didn't really go into any deep details, or story, and exactly, you know, in details. We didn't really talk much about it, you know-- that is, when we were in the-- in the younger years of 10, 11, 12. And when we became more teenagers, we didn't want to talk about it. We started to understand. We said, that's it. What happened happened. We have to build our lives, to start from a new beginning. And that's it. We have to forget about it and start from fresh. So we didn't want to talk about it. So that's-- that's how it was, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today, do you think about your experiences more than you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much more. Much more. And again, especially it started to get to me when I started to get my grandchildren. And when I looked at them, all of a sudden, I started to-- to think about this. I look at them, at their age. Where was I? What did I have? What did I get? How did I live? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have nightmares about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I really-- I really-- I can't say I-- I dreamt a lot about it. I-- I think emotionally, I was-- I was very well, in this way. I was a very strong. I-- maybe because I was-- like, for me, it started when I was 10, when I reached Palestine. It-- like, the whole thing ended-- and maybe because of that. I don't know. But personally, I-- I think I was emotionally very healthy. It didn't leave marks on me-- more now than before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, recently, you went to Poland. How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is something I still can't talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I can say-- we went last year-- no, this year, in May-- April, May. We went to Germany. And well, if it didn't bother me at much, maybe I could understand. But even my wife, even when we were in Bergen-Belsen, it didn't affect her as much as Poland, the memories of Poland. For some reason, we have worse feelings about Poland than about Germany. Because I think we feel that the biggest tragedy-- our biggest tragedy was that the Poles were so much helping the Germans. If-- that they could avoid-- and mind you, I know there were a lot of Poles that they helped Jews. I am not taking this away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as I always say, if somebody tells me, oh, there were thousands of Poles that they helped Jews. I say, so what? I mean, Poland was 34 million people. If there were a few thousand-- 10,000-- what is this about 4 million people? And I guess that may be why it-- it's-- we feel so strongly about it. Is there anything else you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if you want to add anything about my years between '42 and-- and '48, in Israel, I can add to it some-- something. If you want to cut here--

speaker: interviewer
text: If there's anything that you want to-- you'd like to say about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I'll add a little bit of my history in-- in Israel-- Palestine. We came there. Specially, I want to emphasize, like we were talking about, and I mentioned right in the beginning, that my grandmother sending me to that cheder, which my father was opposed, had big [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] results later, when we came to Palestine. And they asked-- and they were asking the children, were you religious at home? Do you want to go to religious institutes or to not religious institute? Even so, I knew very well. I remembered that my father wasn't religious at all. But for somehow, the weight of my grandmother, which I loved dearly-- I can't even mention that name without [DRINKS] getting emotional about it. It made me choose to go to a religion place. And I went. And I went to one place. And I felt that it's not religious enough. And I want to go to more. And they were-- we were in groups of children. And I was 10, at that time. And I organized a group of 10 children, that we came, and we demanded that we want to be transferred to a more religion placing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's stop here.
